---
title: "DEN Data Visualization Color Palette"
subtitle: "v1.0 Design Document"
author:
  - name: "Krisna Gupta"
    orcid: "0000-0001-8695-0514"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Dewan Ekonomi Nasional"
    email: krisna@dewanekonomi.go.id
    corresponding: true
date: 2026-03-29
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

## Introduction

This document specifies the official DEN (Dewan Ekonomi Nasional) data visualization color palette, version 1.0. The palette provides 12 categorical colors organized in three tiers, two colormaps for continuous scales, and usage guidelines for economic charts.

The palette is implemented in three packages:

- **fig-den** (Python/matplotlib, v1.0)
- **ggden** (R/ggplot2, v1.0)
- **den-scheme** (Stata, v1.0)

All three packages use identical hex values in the same order.

## Official DEN Color Identity

Singgih established the official DEN color identity with three colors in the gold-brown hue family (@fig-palette).

![Official DEN color identity.](palette.jpg){#fig-palette width=50%}

| Name       | Hex       | Description           |
|------------|-----------|-----------------------|
| Gold       | `#EEC051` | Primary brand color   |
| Light Gold | `#F8E69B` | Light/pale gold       |
| Dark Brown | `#845B24` | Dark accent           |

: The three official DEN brand colors. {#tbl-official}

These three colors work for logos and letterheads, but **data visualization requires colors that are immediately distinguishable** --- especially in charts with 4--8+ categories. The gold-brown family alone cannot provide sufficient contrast for multi-series charts.

## Design Principles

The palette follows a principle of **minimal departure from the brand**:

1. The three official colors anchor the palette.
2. Additional colors are derived from the gold-brown family (darkening, desaturation, or hue shifts).
3. One non-brown hue is introduced: red (`#C00000`), already used extensively in the DEN chartbook.
4. Every non-official color was validated against actual chartbook usage.

### Color Extraction from the Chartbook

The Directorate for Acceleration of Priority Economic Programs produces a monthly economic chartbook. Colors were extracted from chart pages (pp. 5--28) of the March 2026 edition using PyMuPDF.

| Hex       | Count | Notes                                      |
|-----------|-------|---------------------------------------------|
| `#FFC000` | 259   | Most-used bar fill in entire chartbook      |
| `#935200` | 125   | Dark brown for bars/lines                   |
| `#855209` | 85    | Variant of above                            |
| `#C00000` | 74    | Red accent                                  |
| `#F0A12E` | 37    | Close to old package gold                   |
| `#A4634E` | 26    | Close to old package brown                  |
| `#C17529` | 25    | Orange for tertiary series                  |
| `#91571F` | 24    | Dark amber variant                          |

: Most frequently used colors in the March 2026 chartbook. {#tbl-extraction}

Key finding: the previous package gold (`#F0A22E`) was **not** the chartbook's primary gold (`#FFC000`). The official brand gold (`#EEC051`) sits between them. The v1.0 palette corrects this by using the official brand color as the primary and the chartbook workhorse as a secondary.

![Old package palette (9 colors) vs. actual chartbook colors.](den_palette_comparison.png){#fig-comparison width=90%}

## The 12-Color Palette

The palette has three tiers: simple charts (2--3 series) use the most brand-faithful colors, complex charts (8+ series) draw from the full set.

![The DEN 12-color palette, compared with the official identity (3 colors) and old package (9 colors).](den_palette_proposal.png){#fig-proposal width=90%}

### Tier 1: Core (positions 1--3)

For charts with 1--3 data series.

| Position | Name       | Hex       | Source   | Role                                   |
|----------|------------|-----------|----------|-----------------------------------------|
| 1        | Gold       | `#EEC051` | Official | Primary: first bar/line                 |
| 2        | Dark Brown | `#845B24` | Official | Secondary: second series                |
| 3        | Red        | `#C00000` | Chartbook | Accent: emphasis, negatives            |

: Tier 1, core colors. {#tbl-tier1}

**Why Red at position 3?** Light Gold (`#F8E69B`) is too pale for lines and small elements --- it washes out on white backgrounds. Red provides the contrast needed for a usable 3-color set while remaining warm-toned. It is the chartbook's go-to accent (74 uses).

@fig-line demonstrates the core 3-color set in a line chart.

![Line chart using core colors: Gold, Dark Brown, and Red.](../test/fig/test_line.png){#fig-line width=85%}

### Tier 2: Standard (positions 4--7)

For charts with 4--7 series.

| Position | Name        | Hex       | Source    | Role                                 |
|----------|-------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| 4        | Bright Gold | `#FFC000` | Chartbook | High-saturation gold, bar workhorse  |
| 5        | Tan         | `#A19574` | Derived   | Desaturated brown                    |
| 6        | Dark Grey   | `#3A3A3A` | Essential | Reference lines, baselines, text     |
| 7        | Deep Amber  | `#935200` | Chartbook | Darker variant of official brown     |

: Tier 2, standard colors. {#tbl-tier2}

**Why Bright Gold (`#FFC000`) at position 4?** This is the most-used color in the chartbook (259 occurrences). It is visually close to the official gold but more saturated, making it distinguishable in side-by-side comparisons.

@fig-bar and @fig-bar-multi show bar charts using core and standard colors.

![Grouped bar chart using 3 core colors.](../test/fig/test_bar.png){#fig-bar width=85%}

![Four-category bar chart using core + standard colors.](../test/fig/test_bar_multi.png){#fig-bar-multi width=85%}

### Tier 3: Extended (positions 8--12)

For charts with many categories (trade partners, sectoral breakdowns, etc.).

| Position | Name       | Hex       | Source    | Role                           |
|----------|------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| 8        | Light Gold | `#F8E69B` | Official  | Area fills, lightest bar layer |
| 9        | Warm Sand  | `#C3986D` | Derived   | Midpoint gold-brown            |
| 10       | Rose Brown | `#B58B80` | Derived   | Hue-shifted for variety        |
| 11       | Rust       | `#9F522C` | Chartbook | Distinct warm accent           |
| 12       | Olive Tan  | `#8B8679` | Derived   | Muted neutral                  |

: Tier 3, extended colors. {#tbl-tier3}

**Why Light Gold (`#F8E69B`) at position 8?** As an official brand color it belongs in the palette, but its very light value makes it unsuitable for lines or narrow bars. At position 8, it is used only when many categories require it --- typically as an area fill or the lightest segment in stacked bars, where its lightness is an asset.

@fig-12colors shows all 12 colors in a multi-line chart.

![All 12 palette colors displayed in a line chart.](../test/fig/test_12colors.png){#fig-12colors width=85%}

### Colormaps / Gradients

For continuous scales (heatmaps, choropleths):

- **Sequential:** Light Gold (`#F8E69B`) $\rightarrow$ Gold (`#EEC051`) $\rightarrow$ Dark Brown (`#845B24`)
- **Diverging:** Gold (`#EEC051`) $\leftarrow$ Light Gold (`#F8E69B`) $\rightarrow$ Red (`#C00000`)

**Note on the diverging midpoint:** Light Gold (`#F8E69B`) carries a warm visual bias. In economic charts where the midpoint has strict semantic meaning (e.g., zero growth, trade balance), consider forcing an off-white (`#F5F5F5`) at exactly zero for accurate visual encoding.

## Design Evaluation

### Strengths

The **core 3-color set (Gold, Dark Brown, Red) is excellent**. It provides immediate, unambiguous separation and covers the vast majority of DEN chart output. Gold anchors DEN identity, Dark Brown provides gravitas, and Red draws attention to highlights. This combination is particularly well-suited for policy charts (e.g., Indonesia vs. ASEAN vs. World).

The **brand identity is unmistakable**. The warm gold-brown palette is distinctly institutional and Indonesian --- no one will confuse a DEN chart with a World Bank blue or IMF grey. The overall aesthetic reads as a bespoke publication rather than default software output.

**For 2--4 series charts, the palette is very strong.** @fig-bar and @fig-scatter demonstrate clean, readable output with good contrast.

![Scatter plot using 2 core colors with distinct marker shapes.](../test/fig/test_scatter.png){#fig-scatter width=85%}

### Hue diversity concern at 5+ series

Almost all 12 colors reside in a narrow slice of the color wheel (yellow $\rightarrow$ orange $\rightarrow$ red-brown). The palette contains **zero cool tones** --- no blue, teal, or green. This creates two problems:

1. **Perceptual crowding.** At 5+ series, especially in line charts, the browns/tans/ambers merge into a muddy cluster. Positions 2 (Dark Brown), 5 (Tan), 7 (Deep Amber), 9 (Warm Sand), 11 (Rust), and 12 (Olive Tan) are all in the brown family. @fig-12colors demonstrates that beyond 4 lines, individual series become difficult to identify by color alone.

2. **No semantic contrast pair.** Economic charts frequently need natural warm/cool contrast: exports vs. imports, surplus vs. deficit, growth vs. contraction. The current palette forces these distinctions to be made with "darker warm" vs. "lighter warm," which is perceptually weaker.

### Gold saturation overlap

Positions 1 (Gold, `#EEC051`), 4 (Bright Gold, `#FFC000`), and 8 (Light Gold, `#F8E69B`) are three shades of yellow-gold. In @fig-bar-multi, the NE (pos 1) and West (pos 4) bars can read as "same category, different shade" at first glance. Three golds in a 12-color categorical palette is a luxury that reduces the effective perceptual range.

### Light Gold visibility

Position 8 (`#F8E69B`) is too pale for lines and small markers on white backgrounds. It functions well as a fill color but is nearly invisible as a line, as visible in @fig-12colors (y8).

### Future direction

For a potential v2.0, consider replacing 1--2 colors in the Extended tier (positions 9--12) with muted cool tones. Candidates:

| Current           | Proposed replacement | Rationale                          |
|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------|
| Warm Sand (pos 9) | Slate Blue (~`#4A6D7C`) | Cool contrast anchor            |
| Olive Tan (pos 12)| Muted Teal (~`#5B8A72`) | Second hue family               |

This would give the palette 4 hue families (gold, brown, red, cool) instead of 2 (gold/brown, red) while keeping the DEN identity intact in the Core and Standard tiers. The warm institutional character comes from positions 1--7 --- the Extended tier should prioritize readability.

## Recommended Usage Patterns

| Chart type                    | Colors to use                                 |
|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| 2-series                      | Gold + Dark Brown (positions 1--2)            |
| 3-series                      | Gold + Dark Brown + Red (positions 1--3)      |
| Bar + line combo              | Bars: Gold + Bright Gold, Line: Dark Brown    |
| Stacked bar (4--6 categories) | Positions 1, 4, 2, 7 (light-dark alternation)|
| Many categories (8+)          | Full 12-color palette                         |

: Recommended usage patterns. {#tbl-usage}

**Best practice for 5+ series line charts:** Prefer direct end-of-line labels over legend-only approaches. Because the palette is heavily warm-toned, colors in the brown family are easier to distinguish when labeled directly next to the data rather than matched to a distant legend.

![Pie chart using 4 colors from core and standard tiers.](../test/fig/test_pie.png){#fig-pie width=70%}

## Changes from v0.1

| Position | v0.1               | v1.0                  | Change reason                             |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| 1        | `#F0A22E` gold     | `#EEC051` gold        | Aligned to official brand color            |
| 2        | `#A5644E` brown    | `#845B24` dark brown  | Aligned to official brand color            |
| 3        | `#C00000` red      | `#C00000` red         | Unchanged                                  |
| 4        | `#A19574` tan      | `#FFC000` bright gold | Chartbook's most-used color promoted       |
| 5        | `#3A3A3A` grey     | `#A19574` tan         | Moved back; grey deferred to pos 6         |
| 6        | `#C3986D` sand     | `#3A3A3A` dark grey   | Essential neutral kept                     |
| 7        | `#A0640C` amber    | `#935200` deep amber  | Replaced with chartbook-validated color    |
| 8        | `#B58B80` rose     | `#F8E69B` light gold  | Official color added (was missing)         |
| 9        | `#C17529` orange   | `#C3986D` warm sand   | Orange dropped (too close to golds)        |
| 10       | ---                | `#B58B80` rose brown  | Extended from 9 to 12 colors              |
| 11       | ---                | `#9F522C` rust        | Chartbook-validated addition               |
| 12       | ---                | `#8B8679` olive tan   | Muted neutral for large category sets      |

: Palette changes from v0.1 to v1.0. {#tbl-changes}

**Dropped colors:**

- `#A0640C` (old amber): too close to new Deep Amber and official Dark Brown.
- `#C17529` (old orange): too close to new Bright Gold and Gold.

## Known Limitations

- **Hue clustering:** The palette is heavily warm-toned (yellow--brown--red). For charts with 5+ series, colors in the brown family may be difficult to distinguish. Use direct labeling and consider pattern fills to aid readability.
- **Colorblind accessibility:** The Gold/Bright Gold pair and the Brown/Amber pair may be hard to distinguish for viewers with deuteranopia. Use pattern fills or direct labels when accessibility is critical.
- **12-color ceiling:** Charts with 12+ categories will recycle colors. Group small categories into "Other" or use faceted panels.
- **Light Gold on white:** `#F8E69B` has low contrast on white backgrounds. Works for filled areas, not for lines or small markers.

## Packages

| Package    | Language | Version | Repository |
|------------|----------|---------|------------|
| fig-den    | Python   | v1.0    | [github.com/den-econ/fig-den](https://github.com/den-econ/fig-den) |
| ggden      | R        | v1.0    | [github.com/den-econ/ggden](https://github.com/den-econ/ggden) |
| den-scheme | Stata    | v1.0    | [github.com/den-econ/den-scheme](https://github.com/den-econ/den-scheme) |

: DEN visualization packages. {#tbl-packages}

All packages use identical hex values in the same order.
